coredata.dc:title: Investigation of photocatalytic performance of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite: Effect of π-conjugation
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the photophysics of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and iridium complexes, and their nanocomposites using density functional theory calculations. We evaluate the electronic structures and energy conversion efficiencies of the nanocomposites as a function of the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituents. Our results indicate that the GQD primarily influences the occupied frontier molecular orbitals of the nanocomposite, while the unoccupied frontier molecular orbitals are mainly determined by the Ir(III) complex. The dipolar interaction between GQD and the Ir(III) complex causes a redistribution of charge between the two molecules, resulting in enhanced delocalization of electrons in the nanocomposite. The nanocomposite's unique electronic configuration results in enhanced charge separation, leading to improved electron injection into the semiconductor. Moreover, the high-energy absorption is determined by GQD, while the Ir(III) complex primarily contributes to low-energy absorption. Furthermore, increasing the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent increases the nanocomposite's LUMO levels and open-circuit voltage, but decreases its light harvesting ability. However, the electron-hole recombination time increases, leading to improved short-circuit current and energy conversion efficiency. Our computational results provide valuable insight into the photophysical properties of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites, specifically the important elements affecting their photocatalytic performance.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Driving forces of electron injection and ground-state regeneration of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of GQD − Ir(III) complex nanocomposites with varying degrees of π-conjugation. The 2-aryl-imidazo[4,5-f][1,10]phenanthroline ligand is represented by the molecular fragment encircled by the dashed line. Modifications to the π-conjugated portion of the Ir(III) complex occur at the 2-position of the imidazole ring, denoted by R1, R2, R3, R4, and R5. The difference between R1 and R2 is the position of fusion between two benzene rings, with R1 indicating the meta position and R2 indicating the para position. Similarly, the difference between R3 and R4 is the position of fusion between benzene and phenanthrene rings, with R3 indicating the para position and R4 indicating the meta position. The degree of conjugation decreases in the order of R5 > R4 = R3 > R2 = R1.

The energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The nanocomposites are bound by aromatic substituents with varying degrees of π-conjugation.

Schematic representation of photovoltaic processes in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. Upon photoexcitation of GQD and Ir(III) complex, electrons in GQD are injected into the LUMO of Ir(III) complex, as denoted by (I), while holes in Ir(III) complex are extracted into the HOMO of GQD, as denoted by (II). Meanwhile, intermolecular charge transfer occurs upon photoexcitation of GQD, as denoted by (III), while electrons in the LUMO of the Ir(III) complex are injected into the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2, as denoted by (V). The photoexcited GQD is further reduced by the redox couple I-/I3-, as denoted by (VI). Moreover, electrons in the LUMO of the Ir(III) complex can recombine with the holes in the HOMO of GQD, as denoted by (IV).

(a)-(f) Normalized absorption spectra of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites, as well as the fragment spectra of pristine GQDs (represented by the dark yellow line) and isolated Ir(III) complex (represented by the pink line). The fragment spectra were obtained without any additional geometry optimization of the pristine GQD or isolated Ir(III) complex, which is directly extracted from the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.

Absorption maxima (λmax (nm)), oscillator strength (fosc), interfragment charge transfer (ΔQ (e-)), charge transfer distances of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite (ΔLcom (Å)) and isolated Ir(III) complex (ΔLintra (Å)).

Chemical hardness (α (eV)), chemical potential (β (eV)), electro-donating power (η (eV)), free energies of electron injection (ΔGinj (eV)) and ground-state regeneration (ΔGreg (eV)) of isolated Ir(III) complex and GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the photophysics of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and iridium complexes, and their nanocomposites using density functional theory calculations. We evaluate the electronic structures and energy conversion efficiencies of the nanocomposites as a function of the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituents. Our results indicate that the GQD primarily influences the occupied frontier molecular orbitals of the nanocomposite, while the unoccupied frontier molecular orbitals are mainly determined by the Ir(III) complex. The dipolar interaction between GQD and the Ir(III) complex causes a redistribution of charge between the two molecules, resulting in enhanced delocalization of electrons in the nanocomposite. The nanocomposite's unique electronic configuration results in enhanced charge separation, leading to improved electron injection into the semiconductor. Moreover, the high-energy absorption is determined by GQD, while the Ir(III) complex primarily contributes to low-energy absorption. Furthermore, increasing the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent increases the nanocomposite's LUMO levels and open-circuit voltage, but decreases its light harvesting ability. However, the electron-hole recombination time increases, leading to improved short-circuit current and energy conversion efficiency. Our computational results provide valuable insight into the photophysical properties of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites, specifically the important elements affecting their photocatalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As societies become more aware of the importance of sustainability, research into alternative and renewable energy sources is critical to reducing dependence on non-renewable sources such as fossil fuels. Solar energy, harnessed using photovoltaic cells that convert solar radiation into electrical energy, is one such alternative energy source with great potential. To improve solar energy conversion efficiency, third-generation solar cells are being developed using nanocrystals, polymers, dyes, and concentrated solar cells [1]. In addition, the use of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) and quantum dot solar cells (QDSSCs) is becoming more popular due to their properties such as tunable bandgaps, higher photovoltaic performance, and improved extinction coefficients [2–5]. GQDs or graphene quantum dots, which consist of a conjugated carbon framework, are known for their excellent electrical conductivity and favorable optoelectronic properties [6,7]. Furthermore, GQDs possess unique optoelectronic properties, such as tunable light emission [8,9] and a down-conversion effect [10–12], making them ideal candidates for use in photocatalysis that reduce pollution and promote sustainable energy. Although much research is still being done on photocatalysis, it is critical for the commercialization of these technologies that they are stable and durable.

Recently, researchers have focused on investigating the suitability of QD/dye nanocomposites for energy conversion applications, both experimentally and theoretically. Salam et al. [13] have shown that GQD/N719 dye nanocomposites improve energy conversion efficiency by up to 6.22% compared to N719 dye alone. This improvement was attributed to the optimal energy alignment between GQDs and N719 dye, which promotes electron injection, slows electron-hole recombination, and increases light collection efficiency. It was also found that corn powder-derived GQDs have the potential to downscale UV light to the region that optimally excites the dye sensitizer [10]. In addition, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the dye molecules can be adjusted by modifying spacers and anchors, which offers opportunities to optimize the electron injection in photovoltaic cells. For example, Qian et al. [14] prepared different types of D-π-A molecules with different spacers and linkage groups and found that the 2-(1,1-dicyanomethylene)rhodamine dye exhibited higher electron injection efficiency, resulting in higher short circuit current. Xie et al. [15] found that the addition of a bithiophene bridge increased the short circuit current and energy conversion efficiency of metal-free dyes. The photovoltaic performance of GQD/N3 dye nanocomposites as light sensitizers was also investigated, and the addition of GQDs improved the photoelectric conversion of DSSCs, as reported by Mihalache et al. [16].

Previous experimental work has led to the development of GQD/dye nanocomposites as light sensitizers for photocatalysis. However, a deeper understanding of the electronic structures and charge transfer dynamics between GQDs and dyes is needed to improve their photocatalytic efficiency. Density functional theory (DFT) Research on this topic has included calculations that led to improved light collection efficiency and higher energy conversion efficiency. For example, Wazzan et al. showed that GQD/Oxora flower nanocomposites have higher light collection efficiency, resulting in higher energy conversion efficiency [17]. Incorporation of GQDs into nanohybrids with triazatruxene-based dyes enhanced molecular regeneration and light collection, leading to higher energy conversion efficiency, as found by Zhao et al. [18]. Wang et al. investigated the optoelectronic properties of Pt(II)-coordinated GQDs and found that the phosphorescence emission was improved compared to the Pt(II) complex alone [19]. Jacques et al. investigated the photocatalytic activities of iridium (III)/ruthenium (II) complexes bound both covalently and non-covalently to graphene oxide (rGO) [26]. The resulting nanocomposites exhibited selective photocatalytic activity based on their binding to the rGO.

In recent years, Ir(III) complexes have attracted attention due to their unique photophysical properties [20–23] and their widespread use in photocatalysis [24,25]. The electronic structures of Ir(III) complexes can be easily modified by introducing substituents into their diimine and cyclometallic ligands. Moreover, the Ir(III) complexes exhibit good electron delocalization, which favors charge separation. The most commonly used coordinating ligands for Ir(III) complexes are 2,2'-bipyridine (bpy) and 2-phenylpyridine (ppy), leading to emission lifetimes of microseconds [26–29]. The quantum yield of Ir(III) complexes can be improved by varying the substituents on the ppy and bpy ligands. For example, the emission of [Ir(ppy)2(bpy)]PF6 arises from the interplay of ligand-to-ligand charge transfer (3LLCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (3MLCT), resulting in different emission wavelengths depending on the solvent and host material [30]. Li et al. synthesized seven Ir(III) complexes coordinated to cyclometallating ligands with different degrees of π-conjugation [26]. These Ir(III) complexes exhibit tunable emission wavelengths, lifetimes, and nonlinear optical properties.

Carbon-based materials functionalized with Ir(III) complexes have been shown to be potentially suitable for photocatalytic applications [31–34], but more research is needed to fully understand the underlying mechanisms of their performance. In our previous study [35], we synthesized Ir(III) complexes with good electron delocalization and efficient charge separation [36]. In this study, we focus on the photovoltaic properties of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites with the aim of exploring their potential for photocatalytic applications such as water splitting and organic degradation. We aim to understand the effects of π-conjugation of the substituent on the energy conversion efficiency and electronic interface structure of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites, which play a crucial role in determining their photocatalytic efficiency. In addition, we investigate the role of GQDs in light absorption and energy conversion in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. Our work aims to identify new elements that control the photocatalytic reactions in hybrid light sensitizers, while providing valuable insights into the photocatalytic performance of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.

Five iridium complexes were prepared by adjusting the extent of conjugation of the functional group in the 2-aryl imidazo[4,5-f][1,10]phenanthroline ligand, termed the 2-aryl substituent (see Fig. 1). The five 2-aryl substituents used to form the iridium complexes were naphthalene-1-yl (R1), naphthalene-2-yl (R2), phenanthrene-9-yl (R3), anthracene-9-yl (R4), and pyrene-1-yl (R5). R1 and R2 have a similar degree of conjugation, but differ in the fusion site between the two benzene rings. R3 and R4 have the same degree of conjugation but are larger than R1 and R2 and differ in the fusion site between the phenanthrene and benzene rings. Of all the substituents, R5 has the highest degree of conjugation. In total, we prepared five different iridium complexes, i.e., Ir−R1, Ir−R2, Ir−R3, Ir−R4, and Ir−R5. The binding of GQD to the Ir(III) complex is via an amide bond, which has been shown to be the most stable bond between the dye and GQD in a previous study [37].

The surface of TiO2 is generated by exposing the (101) surface of bulk anatase, which has the lowest surface energy, leading to higher efficiency in DSSCs compared to other surfaces [38,39]. Such a small cluster model of TiO2 can provide an accurate description of the dynamics of charge transfer from the dye sensitizer to TiO2 [18,40,41]. For example, Angelis et al. have studied the optoelectronic properties of a [Fe(CN)6]4-sensitized (TiO2)38 cluster [42]. The absorption energy of the hybrid system and the calculated band gap of the (TiO2)38 cluster in aqueous solution agreed well with the experimental values [43,44]. Therefore, in this study, we used a (TiO2)38 cluster model to represent the semiconductor surface. We have a total of five hybrid systems: GQD/Ir−R1 @ (TiO2)38, GQD/Ir−R2 @ (TiO2)38, GQD/Ir−R3 @ (TiO2)38, GQD/Ir−R4 @ (TiO2)38, and GQD/Ir−R5 @ (TiO2)38. The Ir(III) complex is bound to the surface of (TiO2)38 via a bidentate chelating mode [45,46]. The calculated Ti−O bond lengths of the five molecular systems range from 1.8 to 2.2 Å and agree well with previous results [18,47].

The B3LYP functional [48,49] is used for geometry optimization. The mixed LANL2DZ/6–31 G(d, p) basis set [50,51] is used for Ir and Ti atoms and for nonmetallic elements. The LANL2DZ basis set is a suitable choice for describing the optoelectronic properties of heavy elements [52,53]. B3LYP is able to accurately predict the ground and excited state properties of transition metal complexes, including Ir(III) complexes, and has been used extensively in the literature to study their electronic and optical properties [54,55]. Moreover, B3LYP has been shown to provide reliable results that agree well with experimental data [56–58]. In addition to its accuracy, B3LYP is also relatively computationally efficient compared to more sophisticated methods, making it a practical option for studying large and complex systems such as Ir(III) complexes. B3LYP has also been shown to provide accurate predictions for the electronic structures of various carbon-based materials, including graphene [59,60], carbon nanotubes [61,62], and fullerenes [63,64], and in particular GQDs [65,66]. Considering the accuracy, computational efficiency, and previous success in the study of similar chemical systems, the use of the B3LYP functional system to calculate the optoelectronic properties of the Ir(III) complex and GQDs is a reasonable choice.

The Orca software package [72] with the CPCM model in dichloromethane [67] was used for the calculations. The optimization of the geometric structure was performed without the effect of spin-orbit coupling (SOC), since it has minimal effect in the ligand field environment of the transition metal complex [68]. The resolution-of-identity (RIJCOSX) approximation [69–71] was used to speed up the calculation. In the single-point calculation, the scalar relativistic SCF method was used, which incorporates the Douglas-Kroll-Hess (DKH) Hamiltonian. The relativistically recontracted def2-TZVPP basis set and effective core potentials were used for the Ir and Ti atoms, and the def2-svp basis set was used for the other atoms. The time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations were performed to obtain the excitation energies. In addition, the charge transfer distance (ΔL) and the transferred charges between fragments were calculated using the multiwfn [72].

Fig. 2 shows that the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) of the Ir(III) complexes and GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites are lower than the redox pair I-/I3- (−4.8 eV). Furthermore, all molecular systems have higher LUMOs than the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 (−4.0 eV). Energy conversion applications are suitable for these sensitizers because their energy levels are aligned to ensure fast electron transfer and ground-state regeneration.

The LUMO levels of all Ir(III) complexes remained relatively constant as the degree of π-conjugation of the substituent increased, whereas their HOMO levels increased in proportion to the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent. It is noteworthy that the HOMO levels of Ir−R4 and Ir−R5 were higher than those of the others. Interestingly, although the 2-aryl substituent of Ir−R3 showed a higher degree of conjugation than the substituents of Ir−R1 and Ir−R2, the HOMO level was the same as that of Ir−R1 and Ir−R2 and lower than that of Ir−R4. This suggests that the fusion site between the aromatic rings in the substituent has an effect on the electronic properties of the Ir(III) complex. In contrast, the LUMO levels of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites were more sensitive to the π-conjugation effect, as evidenced by the fact that the LUMOs of Ir−R4 and Ir−R5 were higher than those of the other complexes. However, the effect of π-conjugation on the HOMO levels in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites was weaker compared to that of the Ir(III) complex because the HOMOs of the nanocomposites were determined by the GQD states, which were less sensitive to the conjugation effect due to the large distance between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex. Increasing the degree of conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent resulted in higher LUMO levels in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites and thus increased the charge injection into the semiconductor.

The dipolar nature of the interaction between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex leads to a redistribution of charge between the two molecules, resulting in the HOMO and LUMO of the nanocomposite being controlled by the states of the GQD and the Ir(III) complex, respectively. The effect of π-conjugation on the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) differs in the nanocomposites compared to the Ir(III) complexes alone. The overall effect of the dipolar interaction on the FMOs depends on the nature of the molecules and the specific details of the dipolar interaction. Therefore, enhanced π-conjugation leads to increased HOMO levels and a distinct electron distribution (as shown in Supplemental Fig. S3) of the Ir(III) complexes, whereas it has little effect on the HOMO levels of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites.

Based on the projected density of states (PDOS) analysis (see Supplemental Figs. S1), the frontier occupied molecular orbitals (FOMOs) in Ir(III) complexes are a mixed state of the Ir(III) ion and the ppy ligand, while the frontier unoccupied molecular orbitals (FUMOs) are determined by the bpy ligand (also see Supplemental Fig. S3). The contribution of the 2-aryl substituent to the FOMOs increases with the conjugation degree of the substituent. Although the character of the LUMO remains unchanged after the formation of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites and is still governed by the bpy ligand, the HOMO of the nanocomposite is mainly influenced by the GQD at a low degree of π-conjugation (see Supplemental Fig. S2 (a)-(c)). As the degree of π-conjugation increases, the 2-aryl substituent shifts to higher energy levels, resulting in the FOMOs being governed by a combination of GQD and the Ir(III) complex (see Supplemental Figs. S2 (d) and (e)). The characteristics of FMOs in Ir(III) complexes and GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites indicate that the electron injection sites are always located in the bpy ligands, whereas at a low π-conjugation the redox sites are mainly found in the ppy ligand, while at a high π-conjugation they shift to a mixture of the 2-aryl substituent, the Ir(III) ion and the ppy ligand in the Ir(III) complex and a mixture of GQD and the 2-aryl substituent in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite.

The energy level alignment between the Ir(III) complex and GQD indicates that the latter serves as a co-sensitizer in photovoltaic device. Upon photoexcitation, the excited electrons are injected into the Ir(III) complex (as shown in Fig. 3 (I)), while the excited holes in the complex are extracted into the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the GQD (as depicted in Fig. 3 (II)). The degree of π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent determines the driving force of hole extraction. Since the HOMOs of the nanocomposites are influenced by the GQDs, and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) are determined by the Ir(III) complex, a considerable proportion of charges are delocalized into the complex upon photoexcitation of GQDs, resulting in intermolecular charge transfer (as shown in Fig. 3 (III)). To better illustrate the intermolecular charge transfer, we also performed the analysis of the charge transfer between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex using the Multiwfn software package ( Table 1). The transfer of electrons from GQDs to Ir(III) complexes is 0.00028, 0.00032, 0.00041, 0.00094, and 0.00594 for GQD/Ir(III)−R1, GQD/Ir(III)−R2, GQD/Ir(III)−R3, GQD/Ir(III)−R4, and GQD/Ir(III)−R5, respectively. This suggests that expanding the π-conjugation enhances the adiabatic charge transfer from GQDs to Ir(III) complexes, which benefits the energy conversion of the nanocomposites. The electrons photoinjected into the TiO2 can be transferred back to the Ir(III) complex faster than to the GQD because of the shorter distance between them. Therefore, in this study, we do not consider the electron-hole recombination between TiO2 and GQD.

The nanocomposites comprising the GQD and Ir(III) complex exhibit three significant absorption peaks, as depicted in Fig. 4(a): the first peak appears around 340 nm (S3), the second around 420 nm (S2), and the third around 450 nm (S1) with reduced intensities. Notably, the third peak is absent in the cases of GQD/Ir−R1, GQD/Ir−R2, and GQD/Ir−R3. Based on the analysis of the natural transition orbital (NTO; Supplemental Table S1), the S3 peak in all nanocomposites mainly originates from the intermolecular charge transfer transitions, together with contributions from the π,π * transitions arising from the GQD and 1MLCT transitions. Similarly, the S2 peak primarily arises from the intermolecular charge transfer transitions, admixing with the 1LLCT and 1MLCT transitions and the π,π * transitions originating from the GQD. Finally, the S1 peak mainly consists of the intraligand charge transfer (1ILCT)/1LLCT/1MLCT transitions.

We extracted fragments of pristine GQD and isolated the Ir(III) complex from the nanocomposites without further geometry optimization, and calculated their absorption spectra, referred to as fragment spectra. The fragment spectra show three absorption regions: a high energy region (300–350 nm), a middle energy region (350–400 nm), and a low energy region (400–500 nm). The high energy region is predominantly influenced by the GQD, whereas the middle and low energy regions are controlled by a combination of the GQD and Ir(III) complex. The onset edge of the low energy region is determined by the Ir(III) complex. Notably, the absorption spectra of GQD/Ir−R4 and GQD/Ir−R5 exhibit a redshift in their onset edges, while their main absorption peaks are blue-shifted compared to the other three molecular systems. An analysis of the orbital contributions of excited states reveals that the S3 in GQD/Ir−R4 and GQD/Ir−R5 are primarily composed of HOMO→LUMO transitions, which are governed by the GQD and Ir(III) complex states, respectively (see Supplemental Table S1). Therefore, the larger bandgaps of these two molecular systems cause the blue shift of their high energy peaks. In contrast, the onset edges are mainly determined by the HOMO→LUMO transitions in the Ir(III) complex, and the smaller bandgaps of Ir−R4 and Ir−R5 result in redshifts of the absorption edge.

The order of absorption maxima of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite is similar to that of the isolated Ir(III)complex (Table 1), which is red-shifted with increasing π-conjugation. This suggests that the presence of GQDs does not diminish the positive effect of π-conjugation on the light harvesting ability of DSSCs. Moreover, the absorption maxima of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites are generally shifted towards the blue region compared to those of the Ir(III) complex, except for GQD/Ir−R2, which exhibits a red shift. Nevertheless, the larger oscillator strengths of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites, except for Ir(III)−R2, compensate for the negative impact of the blue shift on the spectra, indicating an improvement in light collection efficiency. Therefore, the extent of π-conjugation and the location of the benzene ring fusion sites affect the absorption maxima of the Ir(III) complex, and attaching GQDs to the Ir(III) complex can mitigate this effect through increasing oscillator strengths. However, the enhancement in oscillator strength depends on the specific fusion site between the benzene rings.

We aimed to investigate whether binding GQD to an Ir(III) complex alters the electron-hole separation upon photoexcitation. To evaluate electron-hole separation, we determined the charge transfer distances of the lowest excited states in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites (ΔLcom; see Fig. 3(III)) and isolated Ir(III) complexes (ΔLintra). Longer charge transfer distances indicate weaker Coulomb interaction between electrons and holes, while shorter distances indicate stronger Coulomb interaction resulting in faster nonradiative recombination [66,73]. Our results revealed that the ΔLcom values for GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites are as follows: GQD/Ir−R2 (4.27 Å) < GQD/Ir−R1 (4.41 Å) < GQD/Ir−R3 (4.45 Å) < GQD/Ir−R4 (4.46 Å) < GQD/Ir−R5 (4.86 Å), while the ΔLintra values for isolated Ir(III) complexes are Ir−R2 (4.26 Å) ≈ Ir−R3 (4.28 Å) < Ir−R1 (4.38 Å) < Ir−R4 (4.55 Å) < Ir−R5 (4.79 Å). Therefore, extending the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent reduces the incidence of non-radiative electron-hole recombination in both the Ir(III) complex and the its nanocomposite. This is due to increased molecular system rigidity resulting from enhanced π-conjugation, which limits nuclear wavefunction overlap and ultimately reduces nonradiative recombination, as described by time-dependent perturbation theory [74]. Notably, the charge transfer distances in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites are larger than those in isolated Ir(III) complexes, indicating that the formation of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanohybrid increases the charge separation in the Ir(III) complex. Moreover, the different fusion positions between the benzene rings affect the charge transfer distances.

The absorption intensity is a key characteristic of a light sensitizer's ability to capture solar radiation. In Ir(III) complexes, oscillator strengths (fosc) follow this order: Ir−R4 (0.19) < Ir−R1 (0.29) < Ir−R3 (0.38) < Ir−R2 (0.61) < Ir−R5 (0.77), in agreement with experimental trends [35]. These results suggest that broadening the absorption range and enhancing light collection efficiency can be achieved by increasing the π-conjugation. The position of the fusion site between the benzene rings affects both λmax and fosc values of the Ir(III) complexes, leading to distinct patterns for Ir−R1 and Ir−R2. Similarly, the fusion site between the benzene and phenanthrene rings can significantly influence the λmax and fosc values, resulting in distinguishable trends for Ir−R4 and Ir−R5. It can be inferred that the light collection efficiency of the Ir(III) complex is sensitive to variations in the fusion site of the aromatic rings, with specific fusion positions between the aromatic rings either increasing or decreasing efficiency. Additionally, attaching GQD to the Ir(III) complex increases oscillator strength with respect to the conjugation degree of the 2-aryl substituent. The difference in the variation trends of oscillator strengths between GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites and Ir(III) complexes can be attributed to increased π-conjugation, which alters the orbital overlap between GQD and the Ir(III) complex, resulting in different probabilities of electronic transition and absorption intensities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Driving forces of electron injection and ground-state regeneration of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
In Fig. 3, it is shown that the photovoltaic efficiency of DSSCs relies on two important factors: electron injection (see Fig. 3 (V);ΔGinj) and ground-state regeneration of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite (see Fig. 3 (VI); ΔGreg). To determine the photocatalytic performance of DSSCs, it is crucial to calculate ΔGinj as follows:

Table 2 shows that the magnitude of |ΔGinj| decreases, while the magnitude of |ΔGreg| increases with extension of π-conjugation in the Ir(III) complex. This suggests that increasing π conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent reduces the electron injection driving force while increasing the ground-state regeneration force of the Ir(III) complex. This trend is attributed to the increased redox potential of the Ir(III) complex with the extension of π conjugation. In contrast, the |ΔGinj| increases with π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite, while |ΔGreg| shows no regular changes. Therefore, increasing π-conjugation of the imidazole ring in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite has a significant effect on electron injection force. The different trend of |ΔGinj| in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite compared to the Ir(III) complex is due to the intermolecular charge redistribution, which alters the characteristics of the FMOs in the nanocomposite. Furthermore, the |ΔGinj| of nanocomposites is greater than that of isolated Ir(III) complex, while the |ΔGreg| becomes slightly smaller. This suggests that binding of GQD to Ir(III) complex increases the electron injection and thus benefits the energy conversion.

The photocatalytic performance of a material is influenced by various factors, including its chemical reactivity. A photocatalyst's reactivity determines its ability to absorb photons and generate electron-hole pairs that facilitate different chemical reactions. Chemical hardness (α), chemical potential (β), and electro-donating power (η) are parameters that are closely associated with the material's chemical reactivity and have a significant impact on its photocatalytic performance. Photocatalysts with lower values of α, β, and η can generate a greater number of electron-hole pairs and exhibit greater energetic stability during charge transfer. In particular, a smaller value of α facilitates electron exchange between the molecule and its environment, while smaller values of β and η enhance the molecule's ability to attract and donate electrons. The calculation details of α, β, and η are provided in the references [75].

Table 2 shows that the α value decreases from the Ir−R1 to Ir−R5, which can be attributed to an increase in the HOMO level. This expansion of the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent decreases the chemical hardness of complex. Furthermore, the β and η values increase, indicating that increasing π-conjugation reduces the charger transfer abilities of the Ir(III) complexes. However, the trends in α, β, and η in the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite are distinct from those observed in the Ir(III) complex. Specifically, the α and β values increase, while the η values decrease with increasing π-conjugation. The variation trends of these chemical reactivity parameters suggest that increasing the π conjugation decreases the molecular hardness and electron-accepting ability but increases the electro-donating ability of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. The distinct trends in α, β, and η observed in the GQD/Ir(III) nanocomposite compared to the Ir(III) complex are due to the charge redistribution, leading to variations in the FMOs of the nanocomposite.

In the presence of SOC, the electronic states of the Ir(III) complex become spin-dependent, and the Newns-Anderson model can be extended to include spin degrees of freedom. This can be done by including additional terms in the Hamiltonian that describe the spin-dependent density of states of the TiO2 surface, i.e., DKH Hamiltonian employed in this study.

where ∆G is the free energy change; V is electronic coupling; β is the attenuation coefficient; A is a constant; r is the distance between ppy ligand and TiO2 surface. The ∆G due to electron-hole recombination was calculated using established methods described in reference papers [18,85,86]. Additional calculation details regarding λ, A and β can be found in the aforementioned reference papers.

Table 3 displays the calculated values of key photovoltaic parameters, including J
sc , FF, V
oc , and related values. The Ƞ-value increases with increasing the degree of π conjugation of Ir(III) complex. As the conjugation degree of the 2-aryl substituent increases, except for GQD/Ir−R5, the electron injection time decreases. The conjugation degree of the π system has two opposing effects on nonradiative charge transfer. On one hand, it increases electron delocalization, leading to more rapid radiationless transitions due to enhanced electron-phonon coupling. On the other hand, it increases molecule rigidity, reducing the number of available vibrational modes and decreasing the overlap between nuclear wavefunctions, thereby slowing down nonradiative transitions. The overall electron injection time is determined by the combined effects of electron delocalization and molecule rigidity. Thus, for GQD/Ir−R5, the rigidity effect dominates over the electron delocalization effect, resulting in a longer injection time. It is also worth noting that the |ΔGinj| value for GQD/Ir−R5 is significantly greater than for the other nanocomposites, implying a much greater electron injection force. The opposite trends of electron injection force with respect to electron injection time in GQD/Ir−R5 can be explained by Marcus theory [87,88]. Nonradiative electron transfer is associated with free energy change, reorganization energy, and electronic coupling. Specifically, the electron injection rate is proportional to the free energy change and electronic coupling. To investigate the electronic coupling effect, we plotted the specific molecular orbital that has a portion of electron density delocalized in TiO2 in the GQD/Ir(III) complex/TiO2 hybrid system (Supplemental Table S2). Our results show that compared to the other nanocomposites, there is barely any electron density localized in the carboxylate linker for GQD/Ir−R5/TiO2, indicating a much smaller electronic coupling between the complex and TiO2 in GQD/Ir−R5/TiO2. The smaller electronic coupling slows down the electron injection to TiO2.

The degree of conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent was found to affect the electron-hole recombination time in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. It is noteworthy that the electron-hole recombination time increases with increasing π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent, except for GQD/Ir(III)−R5. An analysis of the natural bond orbitals (NBO) of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) in these nanocomposites indicated an increasing contribution of the d-orbitals of the Ir(III) complex to the HOMO with increasing conjugation degree of the 2-aryl substituent. During nonradiative charge transfer, the HOMO energy level experiences fluctuations due to the highly sensitive electron density around the metal ion, which can enhance the probability of avoided crossing and increase nonradiative charge transfer rates [89]. Therefore, a higher percentage of d orbitals in the HOMOs of the Ir(III) complex results in faster nonradiative charge transfer rates, while a lower percentage results in slower rates.

The Ƞ-values of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites were found to be much lower compared to values reported in other studies. For instance, Mao et al. reported Ƞ-value of up to 15% using phenothiazine-based organic dyes [90], while Ding et al. reported Ƞ-value of up to 9% using indoline-based D−A− π − A organic sensitizers [91]. This reduction in efficiency is attributed to the cosensitization of GQD, which leads to a reduction in the light harvesting ability of Ir(III) complex in the visible range [92]. In fact, our calculated energy conversion efficiencies of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites are similar to those reported for GQDs by Sharma et al. [80], indicating that the light collection capability of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites is mainly governed by the GQD and is consistent with the fragment spectra presented in Fig. 4(b)-(f). To address this issue, several approaches can be considered. Firstly, increasing the size of GQD can reduce its bandgap and improve light absorption in the infrared region, as demonstrated by Kar et al. in CdX QD/P3HT composites [93]. Additionally, photoelectric conversion in the visible region can be enhanced through heteroatom doping and chemical functionalization [65,94]. Finally, adjusting the binding configuration of GQD and Ir(III) complex can modify the orientation of the transition dipole moment, which can enhance absorption intensity, as shown in a previous study [95].

During photoinduced charge transfer, the electronic properties of excited states are altered, resulting in charge separation and unique responses to light, such as second harmonic generation and other nonlinear optical effects. The concept of total hyperpolarizability is closely linked to this phenomenon, as it indicates a molecule's ability to undergo charge separation when subjected to an electric field, leading to a change in electronic distribution. A molecule with high overall hyperpolarizability is more polarizable and has a more delocalized electron density, making it more susceptible to charge separation under external electric fields, which in turn increases electron injection to semiconductors.

Table 3 displays the total polarizabilities (κ) of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. Those interested in delving deeper into the calculations can refer to reference [36]. The data demonstrate a significant increase in the κ-values of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites compared to the isolated Ir(III) complexes, providing clear evidence that the addition of GQDs to the Ir(III) complex enhances charge separation. This enhancement is due to the unique electronic structure of the interface, which favors charge transfer transitions and subsequently increases electron injection into the semiconductor. Notably, increasing the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent in the nanocomposites results in a decrease in the κ-value, indicating a reduction in electron delocalization. In contrast, the κ-value of the isolated Ir(III) complex increases with increasing π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent. This intriguing difference in the κ-value's changing tendency with respect to the degree of conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent is due to the dipolar interaction between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex, which causes charge redistribution and consequently alters the electron delocalization in the nanocomposite compared to the isolated Ir(III) complex.

This research investigates the photocatalytic performance of GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites by varying the π-conjugation of the 2-aryl substituent using DFT-based calculations. Our findings demonstrate that the energy alignment between GQD and Ir(III) complex enhances charge separation in the prepared nanocomposites. Increasing the π-conjugation affects the HOMO of the isolated Ir(III) complex, while it affects the LUMO of the nanocomposite due to the dipolar interaction between GQD and the Ir(III) complex. Furthermore, the increase in π-conjugation improves the electron injection force, which depends on the explicit fusion position between the benzene rings. Additionally, the binding of GQD to the Ir(III) complex enhances the excited-state charge transfer, resulting in changes in the chemical reactivity of the nanocomposite. Specifically, the π-conjugation extension of the 2-aryl substituent decreases the molecular hardness and electron-accepting ability, while the electron-donating ability of the GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites increases.

The optical transition of the nanocomposite depends on different molecular fragments at different wavelength regions. The high-energy region is governed by the GQD, with the primary absorption peak characterized by a charge transfer transition, while the lower energy region is influenced by the admixture of GQD and Ir(III) complex. The onset edge of the lower energy region is determined by the HOMO→LUMO transition in the Ir(III) complex. The extension of π-conjugation and the fusion location of the benzene rings affect the absorption maxima of the Ir(III) complex, but the attachment of GQDs to the complex can offset this impact. However, the degree of enhancement in the oscillator strength depends on the specific fusion site between the benzene rings in GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposites. Moreover, the charge redistribution that leads to a greater overlap between the molecular orbitals of the fragments enhances the probability of electronic transitions and results in increased absorption intensity, leading to greater light collection efficiency than isolated Ir(III) complex.

Increasing the degree of π conjugation accelerates the electron injection, but decelerates the back electron transfer from the TiO2 to the Ir(III) complex. However, in the case of the nanocomposite with a pyrene-1-yl substituent (GQD/Ir(III)−R5), the Ir(III) ion is less sensitive to the surrounding ligand, resulting in reduced avoided crossing and thus a longer nonradiative charge transfer time. Nonetheless, the larger charge collection efficiency and open circuit voltage compensate for the slower electron injection, leading to the highest energy conversion efficiency in GQD/Ir(III)-R5.
